sources_audited.note: The page defines 334412 as bare rigid or flexible printed circuit boards without mounted components and describes printing, plating, etching, and photoprinting pathways; it supports the boundary and physical-process premise, though not a quantitative obsolescence estimate.

O*NET lists the cited semiconductor-process tasks, including computerized controls, inspection and measurement, photomask work, etching, chemical baths, loading, maintenance, and records. It is an explicitly disclosed occupational process proxy, not PCB labor-share evidence.

IPC's February 2025 release concerns AI-enhanced AOI for PCB assemblies and reports potential improvements in detection accuracy, manual intervention, efficiency, reliability, time to market, and cost, while also noting deployment challenges.

The paper describes a hardware-integrated AI system for automated inspection of PCB-assembly defects and reports improved cycle time relative to manual inspection. The packet correctly treats transfer to bare-board inspection as a proxy.

The 2025 TTM filing documents customized advanced interconnect products, one-stop design and engineering, co-development, compressed lead times, broad end markets, and 2025 sales growth led by aerospace and defense, data-center computing, and networking demand. The packet discloses that this large-company evidence may not represent LMM firms.

The release supports the March 2025 shipment, booking, 1.24 book-to-bill, volatility, and representative North American sample claims. It does not itself evidence the prevalence of service-like LMM programs or AI-value retention, despite also being cited for e and q.

The provider page advertises layout and engineering help, file review and quoting, bare-board manufacturing, short lead times, custom stack-ups, and repeat ordering by prior order or part number. These are provider claims and do not establish representative LMM relationship depth, as the bridge and caveats acknowledge.

Gallup reports fall-2024 survey results on owner age and five-year plans to sell, transfer, go public, close, maintain, or grow; its employer and age splits bracket the packet's 0.16 proxy, but it is not PCB transaction evidence.

caveats: The 0.52 labor-exposure base is not measured from PCB-fabricator task shares: the sources cover a semiconductor occupation and AI inspection of assembled boards, while CAM, quoting, planning, test, maintenance, and customer-support exposure are uncited extrapolations. The broad PROXY label and registered range make this non-material to the current base tier and already estimate-conditioned action.

The rho estimate extends evidence from visual inspection to deployment across quoting, CAM, scheduling, process control, and maintenance without representative bare-board implementation or payback data; this limitation is explicitly disclosed and does not change the MODEL_CONDITIONED action.

The e and q values are inferred from one very large integrated manufacturer and one provider's marketing claims rather than representative LMM prevalence, recurring-revenue, contract, pricing, or retention data. S6 adds demand evidence but no direct support for those two primitives.

The d5 range extrapolates one large company's 2025 growth and a March 2025 industry indicator into five-year real demand for the LMM high-mix subset. The source itself limits book-to-bill interpretation to roughly three to twelve months and warns about monthly volatility.

The favorable high-mix, quick-turn, qualified-program lens is clearly disclosed and coherent, but its economics should not be generalized to commodity or technologically obsolete bare-board manufacturers within the code.
summary: Mechanics pass and every primitive-cited source was reopened. The record is useful and internally coherent, with honest PROXY or ESTIMATE treatment and no defect that would change its PRIORITY base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action. Publication should retain caveats about the lack of representative PCB labor, adoption, LMM program-prevalence, value-retention, and five-year demand evidence.
